summary: Charity Majors distills hard-won lessons from building and running platforms at scale into ten commandments, emphasizing the need to protect the platform from individual users, center observability on user experience rather than system health, and invest in fine-grained operational controls. The advice draws on her experience at Parse and Honeycomb, focusing on what it takes to keep a multi-tenant platform alive and healthy.
key_points: Platforms must protect themselves at the expense of individual apps
Observability should center on user experience, not system-level dashboards
One-off user issues must be turned into support problems, not engineering problems
Fine-grained controls (throttles, blacklists, velvet ropes) are essential survival tools
Dogfooding your own platform is non-negotiable
money_quotes: Any time you have to think about one particular user, you have failed in some way. It doesn't scale. Just a few one-offs a day will drag you down and drown your forward momentum.
It is the job of the platform to protect itself at all costs, including at the expense of your app.

Your observability must center your users' perspective, not your own. The health of the system doesn't matter. The health of every request, and every high-cardinality grouping of requests — those are what matter.
Poorly obfuscated infrastructure decisions are usually less of a threat to your business than befuddled users are.

You will be tempted to disable them because they seem flappy and flaky and need to be fixed. But this is actually what your users are suffering through every day they use your platform. Don't disable them, fix them.
key_insight: Running a platform at scale requires ruthless prioritization, user-centered observability, and the discipline to protect the platform from individual users while giving every user the tools to help themselves.
